summary:
The First Saudi State (Emirate of Diriyya, 1744–1818 AD) was the first political entity ruled by the Al Sa'ud family, established through the alliance between the tribal leader Muhammad ibn Sa'ud of Diriyya in central Arabia and the [[event:2449|Wahhabi]] religious reformer Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab — a politico-religious partnership whose model (Al Sa'ud provides governance; Wahhabi clergy provides legitimacy) has defined Saudi Arabia to the present day. From their initial base in the small Najdi oasis town of Diriyya, the alliance's forces expanded aggressively across the Arabian peninsula, driven by the ideological conviction that only their interpretation of Islam was legitimate and that all others must be corrected by force if necessary.

The First Saudi State's expansion brought it into direct conflict with the Ottoman Empire, which nominally controlled the Hejaz (including Mecca and Medina). Saudi-Wahhabi forces captured Mecca in 1803 and Medina in 1804, imposing Wahhabi practices — destroying domed tombs and shrines (including those over the graves of the Prophet's companions), banning tobacco and music, and enforcing strict religious discipline. These acts were profoundly shocking to mainstream Sunni and Shia Muslims worldwide, who venerated the holy sites. The Ottomans commissioned their most capable subordinate, Muhammad Ali Pasha of Egypt, to retake the holy cities and destroy the Saudi state. After a seven-year campaign, Egyptian forces captured Diriyya in 1818 and destroyed it; the last Saudi ruler Abdullah ibn Sa'ud was taken to Istanbul and executed. The First Saudi State was over, but the Wahhabi-Saudi symbiosis survived, reviving with the Second Saudi State (1823–1891) and ultimately producing the modern Kingdom of Saudi Arabia (founded 1902–1932).
